name: YouTube
overview:
YouTube is the world's largest video platform, hosting billions of videos and serving billions of views per day.
The system must handle massive video uploads (500 hours of video uploaded per minute), transcode videos to
multiple formats and resolutions, serve video streams globally with low latency, support search across billions of
videos, and provide personalized recommendations. Video delivery is the dominant workload, requiring a
globally distributed CDN.

inScope: Transcode to multiple resolutions (360p to 4K) and formats (MP4, WebM)
Search videos by title, description, and tags

items: Support 2B users; 500 hours of video uploaded per minute; 1B hours watched per day
Video start time <2 seconds; 360p version available within 5 minutes of upload
99.99% availability for video streaming — outages affect hundreds of millions of users
CDN cache hit rate >99%; adaptive bitrate must gracefully degrade on poor networks
Search query latency <500ms; recommendations served in <200ms from pre-computed cache
Transcoding pipeline scales horizontally with GPU workers; parallel encoding per quality level

entities.name: Video
entities.schema:
{
  video_id: string,  // 11-char base64 ID
  channel_id: string,
  title: string,
  description: string,
  tags: [string],

  duration_seconds: int,
  view_count: int,
  like_count: int,
  status: enum(UPLOADING|PROCESSING|PUBLISHED|PRIVATE|DELETED),
  uploaded_at: timestamp,
  published_at: timestamp
}

entities.name: VideoChunk
entities.schema:
{
  video_id: string,
  quality: enum(360P|480P|720P|1080P|1440P|4K),
  format: enum(MP4|WEBM),
  chunk_index: int,
  duration_seconds: float,
  gcs_uri: string  // Google Cloud Storage
}

endpoints.response:
Location: <CDN manifest URL>
  (HLS .m3u8 or DASH .mpd manifest)
{ videos: [{ video_id, title, channel, duration, view_count, thumbnail }] }

approaches.title: Single-Server Upload and Transcode
approaches.description: Upload video to a single server; transcode synchronously. A 1-hour 4K video is 50GB. Uploading to a single server takes hours. Transcoding to 6 quality levels takes 6+ hours. Completely infeasible.
approaches.tradeoff: Not scalable; single server cannot handle upload volume.

approaches.title: Distributed Upload + Sequential Transcoding
approaches.description: Resumable upload to GCS. Sequential transcoding pipeline. Resumable upload handles large files. But sequential transcoding (360p → 480p → 720p → ...) takes too long. A 1-hour video takes 6 hours to fully process.
approaches.tradeoff: Sequential transcoding too slow; video not available for hours after upload.

approaches.title: Resumable Upload + Parallel Transcoding Pipeline
approaches.description: Resumable upload to GCS via signed URLs. On upload completion, a transcoding pipeline splits the video into 2-minute segments and transcodes each segment to all quality levels in parallel. 360p version is available within 5 minutes of upload. Higher quality versions follow as transcoding completes. Transcoding workers use GPU instances for hardware-accelerated encoding.
approaches.tradeoff: GPU instances are expensive; parallel transcoding requires complex job orchestration.

approaches.title: MySQL FULLTEXT
approaches.description: Full-text search on video title and description in MySQL. Doesn't scale to billions of videos. No semantic understanding. Poor ranking.

approaches.title: Elasticsearch
approaches.description: Index video metadata in Elasticsearch. BM25 ranking for text relevance. Scales to billions of videos. But doesn't capture semantic meaning (searching 'funny cat videos' doesn't match 'hilarious kitten clips').

approaches.title: Elasticsearch + Semantic Embeddings
approaches.description: Elasticsearch for keyword search. A separate semantic search index (using video title/description embeddings from a language model) for semantic matching. Results from both indexes are merged and re-ranked using a learned ranking model (watch time, CTR, freshness). Video embeddings are pre-computed offline and stored in a vector database (Faiss).
approaches.tradeoff: Vector database adds complexity; embedding computation is expensive; requires recomputing

components.name: Upload Service
components.responsibility: Handles resumable uploads; generates signed GCS URLs; triggers transcoding pipeline

components.name: Transcoding Pipeline
components.responsibility: Splits video into segments; dispatches parallel transcoding jobs; assembles HLS/DASH manifests

components.name: Storage)
components.responsibility: Stores raw uploads and transcoded video chunks

components.name: CDN (Google CDN)
components.responsibility: Serves video chunks globally; adaptive bitrate streaming

components.name: Video Metadata DB
components.responsibility: (Spanner) Stores video metadata; globally consistent; horizontally scalable

components.name: Recommendation Service
components.responsibility: Personalized video recommendations using two-tower model

components.name: View Counter (Redis)
components.responsibility: Approximate view counts using Redis INCR; periodically flushed to

questions.title: 2. Transcoding Pipeline
questions.content:
The transcoding pipeline uses a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of jobs: (1) Split video into 2-minute segments,
(2) For each segment, transcode to 6 quality levels in parallel (GPU workers), (3) Assemble HLS/DASH
manifests from transcoded segments. The pipeline is orchestrated by a workflow engine (Apache Airflow or
Google Cloud Workflows). The 360p version is prioritized so the video is watchable quickly.

questions.title: 3. View Count Accuracy
questions.content:
YouTube displays approximate view counts (rounded to significant figures). View counts are incremented in
Redis (INCR video:{video_id}:views) on each view. Redis counters are flushed to Spanner every 60 seconds.
For viral videos (millions of views/hour), the Redis counter is sharded across multiple keys and summed at read
time. The displayed count is intentionally delayed by a few hours to prevent gaming.

questions.title: 4. Recommendation System
questions.content:
YouTube's recommendation system uses a two-stage approach: (1) Candidate generation: a two-tower neural
network retrieves hundreds of candidate videos from billions using approximate nearest neighbor search, (2)
Ranking: a deep neural network scores each candidate using hundreds of features (watch history, video
metadata, user context). The ranking model is trained to maximize watch time, not just clicks.

questions.title: 5. Copyright Detection (Content ID)
questions.content:
YouTube's Content ID system detects copyrighted content in uploaded videos. When a video is uploaded, it's
compared against a database of reference fingerprints (audio and video fingerprints from rights holders).
Matching is done using perceptual hashing: the video's audio and video are fingerprinted and compared against
the reference database. Matches trigger the rights holder's policy (monetize, block, or track).

design.diagram:
YouTube — System Design
Upload · Transcode · Stream · Recommend

browser/app
Upload Svc
Search/Rec
GCS
raw+chunks
Transcode
parallel GPU
CDN
global edge
Elasticsearch
video search
Rec Svc
two-tower model
Spanner
video metadata
upload video
search/recs
store raw
trigger transcode
store chunks
origin
stream HLS
update metadata
sync to ES
get recs
keyFlows: ① Upload: Client → Upload Svc → GCS (resumable) → trigger Transcode
② Transcode: split into 2-min segments → parallel GPU encode → HLS manifest
③ Stream: Client → CDN (adaptive bitrate HLS) → GCS origin on miss
④ Search: Elasticsearch (BM25) + Faiss (semantic embeddings) → re-rank
⑤ Recommendations: two-tower model (candidate gen + ranking) → Redis cache

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study YouTube's two-stage recommendation system: the candidate generation model (matrix
factorization + deep learning), the ranking model (wide & deep learning), and how they're served at scale.
Expert Note: Learn about video transcoding: the H.264 and H.265 codecs, the trade-off between encoding
quality (CRF) and file size, and how GPU-accelerated encoding (NVENC) works.
Expert Note: Understand HLS and DASH adaptive bitrate streaming: how manifests describe quality
variants, how the ABR algorithm selects quality levels, and how segment duration affects startup latency.
Expert Note: Study perceptual hashing for content fingerprinting: how audio fingerprinting (Chromaprint)
and video fingerprinting work, and how YouTube's Content ID matches fingerprints at scale.
Expert Note: Learn about Google Spanner: how it achieves global consistency using TrueTime, how it
scales horizontally while maintaining ACID transactions, and when to use it vs. Bigtable.
